content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 19 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 109r–110v | **Source Images:** DEU_019_page_001.jpg, DEU_019_page_002.jpg, DEU_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** "When the LORD your God destroys the nations whose land he is giving you, and you take possession of it and settle in their towns and houses, **2** you must set apart three cities for yourselves in the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess. **3** Calculate the distances carefully and divide the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance into three regions, so that anyone who kills another person may flee there.

**4** "This is the provision for the one who kills a person and flees there to save his life: someone who kills his neighbor unintentionally, without having harbored any hatred toward him beforehand— **5** as when someone goes into the forest with his neighbor to cut wood, and as he swings his ax to fell a tree, the head flies off the handle and strikes his neighbor and kills him. That man may flee to one of these cities and live. **6** Otherwise, the avenger of blood might pursue him in a rage, overtake him because the distance is too great, and strike him dead — even though he does not deserve to die, since he did not hate the man beforehand. **7** That is why I am commanding you: set apart three cities for yourselves.

**8** "When the LORD your God enlarges your territory, as he swore to your ancestors, and gives you the whole land he promised them — **9** if you are careful to obey all this instruction I am giving you today, to love the LORD your God and to walk in his ways always — then you must add three more cities to these three, **10** so that innocent blood will not be shed in the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance, and blood guilt will not rest on you.

**11** "But if someone harbors hatred for his neighbor, lies in wait for him, attacks him, and strikes him dead, and then flees to one of these cities, **12** the elders of his own town must send for him, have him brought back from that city, and hand him over to the avenger of blood to be put to death. **13** Show him no pity. You must purge from Israel the guilt of shedding innocent blood, and things will go well for you.

**14** "Do not move your neighbor's boundary marker — the landmarks set up by those who came before you in the inheritance you will receive in the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess.

**15** "A single witness is not enough to convict anyone of any crime or offense they may have committed. A matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.

**16** "If a malicious witness comes forward and accuses someone of a crime, **17** the two parties involved in the dispute must stand in the presence of the LORD before the priests and the judges who are serving at the time. **18** The judges shall investigate the matter thoroughly. If the witness has given false testimony against a fellow Israelite, **19** you must do to him exactly what he schemed to do to the other person. Purge the evil from your midst. **20** The rest of the people will hear about this and be afraid, and they will never again do such a wicked thing among you. **21** Show no pity: life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Cities of refuge — a structured mercy:** The three cities (vv. 1–7) function as asylum zones providing time for due process. The key word is *miqlat* — shelter, asylum. PNEUMA renders this as a living institution: Moses is not just commanding compliance but explaining *why* — so innocent people aren't killed in a passion-driven act of blood vengeance. The system assumes normal human rage ("the avenger of blood might pursue him *in a rage*") and builds safeguards against it.

**"Lying in wait" (v. 11):** The Hebrew *yiqom* and *yasov* carry the sense of ambush and premeditation. PNEUMA renders this as "lies in wait... attacks him" rather than a more neutral description, because the distinction between accident and premeditated hatred is precisely what the passage is adjudicating.

**"Blood guilt will not rest on you" (v. 10):** *Dam naqiy* is literally "innocent blood." The idiom of guilt resting on the community is a communal responsibility concept — when injustice is not corrected, the whole land becomes defiled. PNEUMA renders this in modern terms of moral and legal accountability.

**Boundary markers (v. 14):** The *gevul* (boundary stone) violation is not merely a property crime — it is a violation of inherited covenant land. The phrase "those who came before you" honors the ancestral dimension without literalizing "former ones."

**"Malicious witness" (v. 16):** *Ed chamas* literally means "witness of violence/wrong." The person is not just lying — they are weaponizing the legal system to harm another. PNEUMA renders the spirit: a deliberately harmful, malicious legal attack.

**Lex talionis (v. 21):** The famous formula "eye for eye" here functions as a *cap* on punishment — no more than equivalent retaliation. In context, it closes a passage about false witnesses and proportionality in legal proceedings. PNEUMA preserves the formula as culturally resonant while the context clarifies its limiting function.

*— PNEUMA*
